question: Define an equivalence relation on a set. List and briefly explain the three properties that a relation must satisfy to be considered an equivalence relation.
expectedPoints: Reflexivity: Every element is related to itself (for all a in A, a R a).
Symmetry: If an element a is related to b, then b is related to a (if a R b, then b R a).
Transitivity: If a is related to b and b is related to c, then a is related to c (if a R b and b R c, then a R c).

question: Explain the Fundamental Theorem of Equivalence Relations, which states the connection between equivalence relations and partitions. Describe how an equivalence relation on a set A creates a partition of A, and conversely, how a partition of A induces an equivalence relation.
expectedPoints: An equivalence relation ~ on a set A partitions A into disjoint equivalence classes, where the equivalence class of an element a is [a] = {b in A | a ~ b}. The set of all these classes, A/~, is the partition.
Conversely, given a partition of A, a natural equivalence relation can be defined where two elements are related if and only if they belong to the same partition block.
This establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the set of all equivalence relations on A and the set of all partitions of A.

question: Prove that the relation R defined on the set of integers Z by a R b if and only if 5 divides (a - b) is an equivalence relation. Describe its equivalence classes and the resulting partition of Z.
expectedPoints: Proof of Reflexivity: For any integer a, a - a = 0, and 5 divides 0. Therefore, a R a.
Proof of Symmetry: If a R b, then 5|(a-b). This implies 5|(b-a), so b R a.
Proof of Transitivity: If a R b and b R c, then 5|(a-b) and 5|(b-c). Adding gives 5|((a-b)+(b-c)) = a-c, so a R c.
The equivalence classes are the congruence classes modulo 5: [0], [1], [2], [3], [4], where [r] = { ..., r-10, r-5, r, r+5, r+10, ... } for r=0,1,2,3,4.
This partitions Z into 5 disjoint classes based on the remainder when an integer is divided by 5.
